Tutorial 1: Passive soma

 

 

Goals:

Build single-compartment passive soma model

Learn how to add recording and stimulus electrodes

Try out current clamp and voltage clamp

 

Part 1: Current clamp.

1. Morphology menu: Pick soma morphology. Use default values

 

2. Plots Menu: Add Vm (membrane potential) recording electrode at soma.

 

 

3. Stimulus Menu: Add current clamp electrode (Field = inject) to the soma.

 

Note the expression used to deliver a pulse between t=0.1 and t=0.2 seconds. This is filled in by default to be:

 

1e-11*(t>0.1)*(t<0.2)

 

Here the 1e-11 is the current, in Amps. We multiply this into the condition (t>0.1) which is False (0) below 0.1 seconds, and True (1) above 0.1 seconds. Similarly for (t<0.2).

 

4. Run Menu: When you go to the Run Menu, the display looks like this:

 

The green cone is the recording electrode for the Vm plot. The brown arrow is the stimulus. The blue sphere is the soma. Use right-mouse button to rotate the display.

Click the green “Start” button. The simulation runs and plots the membrane potential.

 

5. File Menu: Put in model information such as model creator and notes, and then Select “Save Model”

 

 

Part 2: Voltage clamp.

We will continue with the previous model.

 

1. Stimulus Menu: Change Stimulus field from “inject” to “vclamp”.

 

Note that the default expression changes since we now control voltage, not current. It becomes

 

-0.065+0.065*(t>0.1)*(t<0.2)

 

The -0.065 is the resting potential in Volts. Initially we hold it at this potential. Between 0.1 and 0.2 seconds, we add 0.065 volts to this, to obtain a potential of 0 Volts. This is our stimulus.

 

2. Plot menu: Add a new plot (the “+”) tab item, so that we retain the plot for the membrane potential. For the new plot select the Field: “vclamp current”. It will automatically assign Relative Path: vclamp.

 

 
 
3. Run menu: Click the green “Start” button. You will now see two plots.

 

 

The upper one is still the membrane potential Vm. It displays the voltage step we are applying. The lower plot shows the current recorded on the Vclamp circuit in the model. Note the capacitive transients and the small holding current offset during the voltage pulse.

 

4. Save this model if you wish. You can also save the values in the plots by hovering over the top of the plot and selecting the download icon.

 

Summary:

Congratulations! You have performed current and voltage clamp recordings on a passive soma. Lessons:

  • •Creating soma using Morphology menu. 

  • •Adding recording electrode (plot). 

  • •Adding stimulus electrode. 

  • •Seeing these things appear in the Setup 3D window as you build them. 

  • •Use mouse to move, zoom, and rotate things in the 3D window. 

  • •Running the model. 

  • •Saving the model. 

  • •Doing it again for voltage clamp. 

 

Things to try next:

  • •Different morphologies. 

  • •Different stimulus patterns. You can even use trig functions if you like. 

  • •Zoom and pan on the plots. Select options from the list that appears when you hover. 

  • •More than one stimulus or plot, say on a ball-and-stick model. 

 

 

 

What it does:

 

Jardesigner builds and runs neuronal models which incorporate both electrophysiology and chemical signaling.

 

Ephys:

 

Morphologically detailed models with HH-format ion channels, synaptic channels and simple Ca dynamics.

 

Chem:

Reaction-diffusion chemistry with reactions, mass-action and Michaelis-Menten enzymes, and transport/pores between compartments.

Simulation control:

Stimuli, plots, 3D displays, file I/O and other things needed to build, run and save models.

Standard format and database imports:

Jardesigner reads standard formats and talks to databases which host resources for morphology, ephys, and chemistry. Our database coverage is not exhaustive, but given a simple database API we can add more.

Type of resource

File formats

Databases

Neuronal morphology

.swc, .xml (neuroML)

NeuroMorpho.org, Allen Brain

Channel model definitions

.xml (neuroML)

ICG: Ion Channel Genealogy

Chemical models

.g, .xml, .sbml (SBML)

DOQCS, BioModels

 

Documentation and Tutorials:

The Jardesigner project is developing documentation (what you see now) and has numerous tutorials as part of this documentation.

 

How it works:

Jardesigner does number-crunching and model building on a server, and all the user-interface operations work within your browser.

There is an official Jardesigner server at https://www.jardesigner.org/

Anyone can access this to run their simulations.

You can also run Jardesigner entirely on your own machine, if you want to handle the server locally or offline.

You can also dispense with the GUI entirely and use the jardesigner python library in scripts of your own.

Server:

All computations run on the server, and invoke MOOSE ( https://www.mooseneuro.org/ ) to do the number crunching. MOOSE is a C++ program with a Python interface.

Each session has an independent directory on the server which is deleted as soon as the session ends. No cookies, no login, no data retention.

GUI:

The GUI is implemented in JavaScript. The entire interface and 3D display run in your browser. When you click on the Jardesigner link, the server sends the interface to your system. Once loaded, the interface and server send information back and forth as you build your model.

Build-on-close:

The Jardesigner uses the Build-on-close approach. This means that the model is entirely rebuilt on the server every time you close a menu box, or open a new one. This has the following implications:

  • •It is not instantaneous. Many React interfaces update everything with every keystroke. Jardesigner does not. This is because the model rebuilding is a lot of work. 

  • •There is no final compile step. You know as soon as you close a menu if the entire model was built successfully. 

  • •There is a record of every change you make. You can download this. 

  • •Jardesigner provides an iconified representation of every part of your model as the building progresses. This is in the Setup3D display. 

 

With that overview, let’s dive into an example model.
